← the Atlas

Remote terminals — the finale (R9–R10)

budding·proposed·JUN 28, 2026

The corrected R9–R10 plan. The awareness capability is ALREADY a decoupled library (@kolu/terminal-workspace — its dependency arrow points out, six consumers), and kolu already consumes it in-process. The earlier "kolu runs a local pulam process" correction (PR

Contents

  • Roadmap
  • What R9 reuses — and what it must build
  • The sub-phases
    • R9·lib — consolidate the pulam-library (one assembly, lib↔daemon 1:1) · the prerequisite
    • R9.0 — kolu consumes the pulam-library in-process · the decouple completes
    • R9.1 — the TerminalEndpoint resolver — shipped
    • R9.2 — ssh kaval driver → a remote PTY tile
    • R9.3 — remote awareness = mirror the remote pulam daemon
    • R9.4 — persisted location, reconnect, adoption
    • R9.5 — the Code-tab fs/git rewrite (procedure + pulse)
    • R10 — canvas multiplex + validate
  • The shape after R9.0
  • Open decisions

The last leg of #951 — kolu dials remote hosts. R8 (#1594) shipped the right reader-join (a terminal is kolu.authored ⋈ terminalWorkspace.awareness, joined at the reader) — keep that. The question this note settles is how kolu gets the awareness half for a remote host, and the answer turns out to already be in the repo: the awareness capability is a decoupled library — @kolu/terminal-workspace — and kolu already consumes it in-process. The only real defect is that the library is a bag of pieces with no single assembly, so the daemon and kolu each wire those pieces up themselves, divergently. This note corrects course: give the library one assembly entry point (a working name: createPulam), make the pulam daemon a trivial wrapper over it, have kolu consume that same assembly in-process for the local box (R9.0) and mirror the remote pulam daemon for a remote host (R9.3). The decoupling is the library boundary, not a process boundary. The parent remote-terminals keeps the one-line rows; full model in the terminal model.

Superseded — split deriving from storing first (S1 · S2)

A first-principles step back found the createPulam framing in this note still shares the wrong thing. The root complect is that the sensor engine derives awareness by mutating a host record it also reads back — deriving is fused with storing — which is what forces “two assemblers” and the fold-clobber in the first place. Awareness — split deriving from storing proposes splitting them (S1: a pure engine; S2: a host-owned fold), after which R9·lib dissolves, R9.0 is a trivial clobber-free loop, and R9.3 is mirror + one reconcile. That note carries the types, API, and interaction diagrams to evaluate the boundary. The createPulam plan below is kept for context but superseded by it.

The earlier correction was itself a misdiagnosis — R9.0-as-a-process is CLOSED

The prior plan (and PR #1614) said R8’s mistake was that kolu serves awareness in-process, and proposed kolu run a local pulam process it mirrors over a socket. That was wrong on the diagnosis and the cure. Grounded in the code: @kolu/terminal-workspace already points its dependency arrow out (it names no kolu-app package; its lone host coupling is an injected logger) and already has six consumers — it is the decoupling. kolu already consumes it in-process (server/src/terminalEndpoint/local.ts runs the library’s startAwareness against kaval’s taps). So “decouple awareness from kolu in code” was solved before R9.0. The local-pulam-process bought no decoupling and introduced a fold-clobber blocker that is intrinsic to mirroring an ephemeral local process across a socket. PR #1614 is CLOSED (never merged); the process approach is rejected. The pulam daemon is kept for the remote case, where a process boundary is real.

The real gap — no 1:1 between the library and the daemon

The library exposes the parts — the sensor engine (startAwareness) and the sink interface (AwarenessSink), the kaval-tap bridge (bridgeKavalTaps), the schema/seeds, the served-surface factory (serveTerminalWorkspace), the surface contract, the fs/git endpoint. It does not expose the whole: “given a dialed kaval, produce a live, served terminalWorkspace surface.” That assembly lives in the pulam daemon (pulam/src/daemon.ts), along with two generic pieces stranded there — the per-terminal sink (pulam/src/hooks.ts) and the byte-tap activity tracker (pulam/src/activity.ts). kolu re-implements a different subset of the same assembly inside local.ts. So there are two assemblers, assembled differently — the root of the duplications and the local-vs-remote divergence. Closing the gap is R9·lib below.

Roadmap

FINALE — R9 (corrected) · R10✓ shipped · ▶ do next · ◐ build-clean · ○ todo · → drill in
▶R9·lib · consolidate the pulam-library — one assembly (createPulam), lib↔daemon 1:1the prerequisite · needs R6 · R4
○R9.0 · kolu consumes the pulam-library in-process (the decouple completes)needs R9·lib
✓R9.1 · the TerminalEndpoint resolver (orthogonal — shipped)#1603
○R9.2 · ssh kaval driver → a remote PTY tileneeds R9.1
○R9.3 · remote awareness = mirror the remote pulam daemonneeds R9.0 · R9.2 · R7
○R9.4 · persisted location + reconnect + adoptionneeds R9.2
○R9.5 · Code-tab fs/git → procedure + pulseneeds R-pulamweb-4 · R9.3
○R10 · canvas multiplex + validateneeds R9.5

R9·lib is the new foundation — it makes the library own the whole awareness assembly so the daemon and kolu stop re-deriving it. R9.0 then becomes small: kolu consumes that one assembly in-process. R9.1 shipped (the PTY-endpoint path, orthogonal to awareness). R9.3 is symmetric to R9.0 — the same surface, mirrored over the wire instead of called in-process. The earlier optional “R9-converge” phase is dropped (it presupposed a local pulam socket for pulam-web to dial; there is none in the in-process model, and pulam-web already keeps its own pulam).

What R9 reuses — and what it must build

Shipped seams (reuse as-is):

seam where leg it serves
serveTerminalWorkspace @kolu/terminal-workspace the surface-skeleton factory both homes already call — the awareness + activity backing injection point
startAwareness · AwarenessSink · bridgeKavalTaps @kolu/terminal-workspace the transport-agnostic sensor engine, sink interface, and kaval-tap bridge — the pieces R9·lib assembles into one entry point
the pulam daemon pulam (R4) the one sensor set per host; serves terminalWorkspaceSurface standalone — kept for the remote case
frontDaemonOverStdio @kolu/surface-daemon dial a remote kaval over ssh stdio → the remote PTY
getHostSession @kolu/surface-nix-host a pooled, long-lived session to dial a remote pulam → the mirror
mirrorRemoteSurface @kolu/surface the total dual ({ procedures, done }, R7) — mirror a remote pulam’s surface into one local handle
HostLocation remote arm · persisted location @kolu/common { kind:"remote", hostId }, round-trips to disk

Must build:

gap today the phase
one library assembly entry point (createPulam) + move the sink and byte-tap into the library the assembly + sink + tracker live in the pulam daemon; kolu re-implements a divergent subset R9·lib
kolu consumes that assembly in-process (drop kolu’s hand-rolled sink + inline tap bridge) kolu wires startAwareness itself in local.ts R9.0
the ssh driver (the remote PTY arm) only {kind:"local"} exists R9.2
mirror a remote pulam daemon’s surface as kolu’s remote awareness backing — R9.3

The sub-phases

R9·lib — consolidate the pulam-library (one assembly, lib↔daemon 1:1) · the prerequisite

R9·lib· one library assembly entry point — createPulam; the daemon wraps it○ todo
needs ← R6 · R4

The gap, grounded. The library (@kolu/terminal-workspace) is genuinely decoupled — its dependency arrow points out (package.json: @kolu/surface, kaval, the agent/git integrations; no kolu-app dependency) and it has six consumers (kolu-server, pulam, pulam-web, pulam-tui, common, solid-statepip). But it exports parts, not a whole:

  • the sensor engine startAwareness and the interface AwarenessSink (sensors.ts:849, sensors.ts:163),
  • the kaval-tap bridge bridgeKavalTaps (kavalChannels.ts:58),
  • the surface-skeleton factory serveTerminalWorkspace + quietActivity (serveTerminalWorkspace.ts:73, serveTerminalWorkspace.ts:53),
  • the surface contract terminalWorkspaceSurface and the fs/git endpoint createTerminalWorkspaceEndpoint.

What it does not export is the assembly that turns a dialed kaval into a live, served terminalWorkspace surface. That assembly lives in the pulam daemon (pulam/src/daemon.ts:114 runPulamDaemon): dial kaval → per-terminal watchTerminal (daemon.ts:203) wires seedAwarenessValue → its own makeAwarenessSink → bridgeKavalTaps → startAwareness → a raw-output activity tap → and reconcile (daemon.ts:282) keeps the set in step, all fed into serveTerminalWorkspace. Two generic pieces are stranded in the daemon package: the per-terminal sink makeAwarenessSink (pulam/src/hooks.ts:39) and the byte-tap createActivityTracker (pulam/src/activity.ts:43).

kolu re-implements a divergent subset of that same assembly:

piece the library / daemon kolu-server the duplication
the sink pulam/src/hooks.ts:39 (publishes the whole value into the served collection) local.ts:278 (writes the registry projection · trackRecentRepo/trackRecentAgent · screen-scrape off the local handle) two impls of one AwarenessSink interface
the kaval-tap bridge bridgeKavalTaps (kavalChannels.ts:58) the four channels built inline (local.ts:688-744) kolu reaches kaval over the same socket-backed ptyHostClient facade (local.ts:4), so its inline copy collapses onto bridgeKavalTaps — re-adding its own signals.cwd consumer for the persisted-cwd write (local.ts:716), exactly as the daemon does (daemon.ts:237); the library’s docstring (kavalChannels.ts:6) still calls kolu’s feed “in-process,” now stale
live activity createActivityTracker (pulam/src/activity.ts:43), served live quietActivity (surface.ts:458); the green dot comes from client-side useTerminalActivity one byte-tap in the daemon, a second on the client; kolu’s server-side activity is quiet

The fix — one assembly, two trivial homes. Lift the assembly into the library behind one entry point — a working name, createPulam(kavalClient) — that owns the per-terminal sensor lifecycle (seed → sink → tap-bridge → sensors → activity tap → reconcile) and returns the serveTerminalWorkspace deps, with the sink’s write target injected by the home (the same shape serveTerminalWorkspace already uses to inject the awareness backing). Move makeAwarenessSink and createActivityTracker into the library. Then:

  • the pulam daemon collapses to a trivial shell — it dials a kaval, builds createPulam(kaval), and serves the result over a unix socket (or stdio, for ssh);
  • kolu calls createPulam against its own kaval client, in-process, injecting “write into the registry record” as the sink target — so its persisted/live fence and the reader-join are unchanged (R9.0).

One assembler, in the library; the daemon and kolu are both thin. That is the 1:1 — library ↔ daemon — and it is what makes R9.0 (in-process) and R9.3 (mirror the daemon) two faces of the same surface.

Open design calls this phase pins (don't invent them mid-flight)

  • Where the in-process sink writes. kolu’s awareness must keep landing on its registry entry (so composeTerminalMetadata, the persisted/live fence, and session-restore hold). createPulam must take the sink’s write target as an injected dep — registry-mutation for kolu, an owned store for the daemon — exactly the injection serveTerminalWorkspace already does for the awareness backing. No fold: in-process the sink mutates the restored record directly, with no socket reseed.
  • The per-terminal lifecycle. kolu’s lifecycle is entangled with spawn/sleep/wake/adopt (local.ts, ~1100 lines); the daemon’s is a plain poll-list reconcile. The minimum 1:1 is one sink + one tap-bridge + one activity tracker in the library, both homes assembling from those. Whether to also lift the whole watch/reconcile loop into createPulam (vs leave kolu’s lifecycle as the caller of the shared pieces) is the scope call this phase makes.
  • Activity. Decide whether kolu’s server-side activity source goes live off the shared byte-tap (retiring the client-side useTerminalActivity) or stays client-side. This phase only moves the tracker into the library; flipping kolu’s source live is a follow-on, not a freebie.
  • Rename. terminal-workspace → pulam-library — the library is the pulam capability; the daemon is one deployment of it. The rename rides this phase (mechanical, ~6 consumers).
  • Needs: R6 (the library exists) and R4 (the pulam daemon) — both shipped.
  • Done: there is one assembly entry point in the library; the pulam daemon is a thin serve-wrapper over it (no awareness logic of its own); makeAwarenessSink and createActivityTracker no longer live in the pulam package; a differential test pins the daemon’s assembly and kolu’s in-process one as the same code path.

R9.0 — kolu consumes the pulam-library in-process · the decouple completes

R9.0· kolu consumes the pulam-library in-process○ todo
needs ← R9·lib

The point. Once R9·lib gives the library one assembly entry point, kolu’s local awareness is just createPulam against its own kaval client, called in-process — no daemon, no socket, no mirror, no fold. kolu deletes its hand-rolled sink (local.ts:278) and its inline tap-bridge (local.ts:688-744) and consumes the library’s assembly instead, injecting “write into the registry record” as the sink target. The reader-join stays exactly as R8 shipped it: the browser still subscribes to terminalWorkspace.awareness ⋈ kolu.authored and joins via composeTerminalMetadata. The arrow points into the library, in-process — which is not re-coupling, because the library knows nothing of kolu (its dependency arrow points the other way).

This is the in-process face of the same surface R9.3 mirrors remotely. It does not start a second process, and it does not change what the browser reads — only how kolu’s awareness collection is backed (now one library assembly rather than a bespoke in-line wiring).

  • Migration: a hard cutover — one change deletes kolu’s bespoke sink + inline bridge and routes through the library’s assembly, no flag and no fallback (the no-knobs rule; the worst case on the local awareness path is a briefly-stale badge, never lost work).
  • Needs: R9·lib.
  • Done: kolu-server runs no bespoke awareness assembly — its local awareness is the library’s createPulam assembly called in-process; the bespoke sink and inline tap-bridge in local.ts are gone; the reader-join and disk persistence are unchanged; a test pins kolu’s in-process awareness equal to what the pulam daemon would serve for the same terminals.

R9.1 — the TerminalEndpoint resolver — shipped #1603

R9.1· HostLocation-keyed endpoint resolver✓ shipped
needs ← R8links #1603

The PTY-endpoint path — orthogonal to awareness, so the correction above doesn’t touch it. resolveTerminalEndpoint is the one HostLocation-keyed seam mapping a terminal to its kaval endpoint; the {kind:"remote"} arm fails loudly until R9.2 fills it (.exhaustive() forces a deliberate arm). Per-terminal ops resolve off entry.meta.location, so R9.2 is a one-case addition.

R9.2 — ssh kaval driver → a remote PTY tile

R9.2· ssh kaval driver — remote PTY○ todo
needs ← R9.1

Add the {kind:"remote",hostId} arm to the resolver: dial the remote host’s kaval over frontDaemonOverStdio (the same ssh <host> kaval --stdio relay kaval-tui --host uses) → a remote PTY on the canvas, PTY only (no awareness badge yet). Persist location = {kind:"remote",hostId}. Adoption reuses the local boot’s adoptOrEnsure spine + the pid-gate’s atomic link(2) (a dial joins a host’s kaval, never races to kill it).

  • Needs: R9.1.
  • Done: dial a host, spawn a PTY, see an ssh-badged remote tile that echoes; it survives a network blip via re-attach. e2e: a remote PTY round-trips over ssh.

R9.3 — remote awareness = mirror the remote pulam daemon

R9.3· remote awareness — mirror the remote pulam daemon's surface○ todo
needs ← R9.0 · R9.2 · R7

A remote host runs the pulam daemon — which, after R9·lib, is the same library assembly (createPulam) served over a socket. So kolu’s remote awareness backing is just a mirror of that daemon’s served surface, dialed over a long-lived getHostSession and mirrored via R7’s total dual. It is the wire-side face of the same surface kolu consumes in-process locally (R9.0): the contract (terminalWorkspaceSurface), the reader-join, the authored half, and disk persistence are identical — only the source differs (an in-process call vs a mirror over ssh). The remote tile gains the git/agent/PR badge + green dot; activity rides the mirror.

This is also where the genuinely remote-only reconciliation lives: a restored remote tile’s persisted awareness (git, lastActivityAt) must survive a fresh daemon re-deriving it across the wire. That fold belongs here, on the remote backing, not in the local in-process path (where there is no socket and no reseed).

  • Needs: R9.0 (the in-process consumer proves the surface), R9.2 (the remote PTY), R7 (the total dual).
  • Done: a remote tile shows live awareness mirrored from the remote pulam daemon, joined by the same reader-join as local; a differential test pins it equal to the remote pulam’s own awareness.

R9.4 — persisted location, reconnect, adoption

R9.4· persisted location + reconnect + adoption○ todo
needs ← R9.2

Harden the remote lifecycle: re-validate the persisted location on reconnect, and cover the adoption/reconcile cases.

the remote kaval is… kolu does
absent provision + spawn fresh (adoptOrEnsure → ensure)
live · wire-compatible adopt — connect, never kill; its PTYs reconcile in
live · a build behind adopt + an “update pending” nudge
live · contract-skewed recycle (kill → respawn) — only on a typed DaemonContractSkewError
unreachable bounded retry, then degraded — never killed
  • Open (decide here): what is re-validated on reconnect and the fail behavior. location is the host discriminator (kolu.authored); cwd is the observed dir (terminalWorkspace.awareness) — different collections. Spell out the comparison and the fail-fast (crash loudly vs re-derive); don’t invent it at implementation time.
  • Needs: R9.2.
  • Done: a kolu-server restart re-adopts remote PTYs; each adoption-table row has a test.

R9.5 — the Code-tab fs/git rewrite (procedure + pulse)

R9.5· Code-tab fs/git → procedure + pulse○ todo
needs ← R-pulamweb-4 · R9.3

The one large client change, and the only finale phase R-pulamweb-4 gates. The Code tab reads koluSurface’s value-bearing streams today (CodeTab.tsx — app.streams.gitStatus/fsListAll/gitDiff.use). Rewrite it to the shared surface’s procedure + {seq} pulse: call the procedure once, re-query on each pulse (the two wire shapes are in surface live data). This deletes the last koluSurface fs/git reads and works for local and remote tiles alike.

  • Needs: R-pulamweb-4 (proves the procedure + pulse remote leg over websocketLink + surfaceClient), R9.3.
  • Done: the Code tab shows live git status/diff for a remote tile via procedure + pulse; no koluSurface fs/git reads remain.

R10 — canvas multiplex + validate

R10· canvas multiplex + validate○ todo
needs ← R9.5

The UI: an active-kaval strip (local always present), a per-tile host hint, an ssh-config host picker — kinda like tmux sessions. Then validate the feel on real use: does N-kavals-at-once beat tmux-style one-at-a-time switching? Fall back to switch if multiplex doesn’t earn its keep. The granularity call lands here, validated, not up front.

  • Needs: R9 (all of it).
  • Done: the validated UX call, recorded.

The shape after R9.0

Open decisions

Resolve each before the phase that needs it — so the implementer isn’t choosing mid-flight.

decision needed by options
where the in-process sink writes (the injected write target) R9·lib registry-mutation for kolu vs an owned store for the daemon — the same injection serveTerminalWorkspace already does for the awareness backing
how much of the per-terminal lifecycle createPulam owns R9·lib minimum (shared sink + tap-bridge + tracker, kolu keeps its lifecycle as caller) vs full (lift the watch/reconcile loop too)
kolu’s activity source after the tracker moves R9·lib / R9.0 go live off the shared byte-tap (retire client-side useTerminalActivity) vs keep it client-side
rename terminal-workspace → pulam-library R9·lib mechanical (~6 consumers); the library is the capability, the daemon a deployment
reconnect re-validation semantics R9.4 what’s compared (location vs the remote kaval’s live cwd) + the fail-fast behavior

Referenced by

  • Awareness — the producer observes, kolu remembers
  • Remote terminals over SSH
  • The Terminal Model — two collections, one reader-side join (and what to name them)
